content.content:
# GitHub Pull Request API: Fields and Endpoints

## Available Endpoints

1. **List pull requests** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls`
2. **Create a pull request** - `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls`
3. **Get a pull request** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}`
4. **Update a pull request** - `PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}`
5. **List commits on a pull request** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/commits`
6. **List pull request files** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/files`
7. **Check if merged** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/merge`
8. **Merge a pull request** - `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/merge`
9. **Update PR branch** - `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/update-branch`

## Pull Request Object Fields

**Core Fields:**
- `url`, `id`, `node_id`, `html_url`, `diff_url`, `patch_url`
- `number`, `state`, `locked`, `title`, `body`
- `created_at`, `updated_at`, `closed_at`, `merged_at`

**Merge-Related Fields:**
- `merged`, `mergeable`, `rebaseable`, `mergeable_state`
- `merge_commit_sha`, `merged_by`
- `auto_merge` (contains `enabled_by`, `merge_method`, `commit_title`, `commit_message`)

**Change Metadata:**
- `additions`, `deletions`, `changed_files`, `commits`

**Status & Drafts:**
- `draft`, `author_association`

**User & Team References:**
- `user`, `assignees`, `requested_reviewers`, `requested_teams`

**Organization Items:**
- `labels`, `milestone`

**Repository References:**
- `head` (branch info), `base` (branch info)

**Other Fields:**
- `active_lock_reason`, `maintainer_can_modify`, `review_comments`, `comments`

# Pull Request Review Fields

Based on the GitHub REST API documentation, here are the available fields for pull request reviews:

**Core Identity Fields:**
- `id` (integer) - Unique review identifier
- `node_id` (string) - GraphQL node identifier
- `review_id` (integer) - Used when referencing the review in API paths

**Content Fields:**
- `body` (string) - Main review text
- `body_html` (string) - HTML-rendered body
- `body_text` (string) - Text-only representation

**Status & State:**
- `state` (string) - Review status (APPROVED, REQUESTED_CHANGES, COMMENTED, PENDING, DISMISSED)
- `submitted_at` (date-time) - When the review was submitted

**User Information:**
- `user` (Simple User object or null) - Reviewer details including login, avatar_url, html_url, and type
- `author_association` (enum) - Relationship to repository (OWNER, MEMBER, CONTRIBUTOR, etc.)

**Code Context:**
- `commit_id` (string or null) - SHA of the commit being reviewed
- `pull_request_url` (string) - Associated pull request URL

**Navigation:**
- `html_url` (string) - Web interface link
- `_links` (object) - Related resource links

The documentation notes that "Pull Request Reviews are groups" of comments grouped with state and optional body text.

# GitHub Issues REST API: Fields and Endpoints

## Issue Object Fields

**Core Properties:**
- `id`, `node_id`, `number`, `url`, `html_url`
- `title`, `body`, `body_text`, `body_html`
- `state`, `state_reason` (completed, reopened, not_planned, duplicate)
- `created_at`, `updated_at`, `closed_at`

**Organization & Ownership:**
- `user` (Simple User object)
- `repository_url`, `repository` (full Repository object)
- `author_association` (OWNER, COLLABORATOR, CONTRIBUTOR, MEMBER, etc.)
- `performed_via_github_app` (GitHub App object or null)

**Assignment & Tracking:**
- `assignees` (array of Simple User objects)
- `labels` (array of label objects)
- `milestone` (Milestone object or null)
- `locked`, `active_lock_reason`
- `comments` (integer count)

**Issue Relationships:**
- `pull_request` (object with diff_url, html_url, patch_url, merged_at)
- `parent_issue_url`, `sub_issues_summary` (total, completed, percent_completed)
- `issue_dependencies_summary` (blocked_by, blocking counts)

**Additional Data:**
- `reactions` (Reaction Rollup: +1, -1, laugh, confused, heart, hooray, eyes, rocket)
- `pinned_comment` (Issue Comment object or null)
- `closed_by` (Simple User object or null)
- `type` (Issue Type object)
- `draft` (boolean)
- `timeline_url`
- `issue_field_values` (array of custom field values)

## Available Endpoints

| Method | Endpoint | Purpose |
|--------|----------|---------|
| GET | `/issues` | List authenticated user's issues across all repos |
| GET | `/user/issues` | List issues in owned/member repos assigned to you |
| GET | `/orgs/{org}/issues` | List organization issues assigned to you |
| GET | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues` | List repository issues |
| GET | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}` | Retrieve specific issue |
| POST | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues` | Create new issue |
| PATCH | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}` | Update issue |
| PUT | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/lock` | Lock conversation |
| DELETE | `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/lock` | Unlock conversation |

# GitHub REST API Commits: Complete Field and Endpoint Reference

## Endpoints

### 1. List Commits
**GET** `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits`

Returns an array of commits from a repository.

**Query Parameters:**
- `sha` (string) – Starting point for commit list (defaults to default branch)
- `path` (string) – Filter by specific file path
- `author` (string) – Filter by GitHub username or email
- `committer` (string) – Filter by committer username or email
- `since` (string) – ISO 8601 timestamp for lower bound
- `until` (string) – ISO 8601 timestamp for upper bound
- `per_page` (integer) – Results per page, max 100 (default: 30)
- `page` (integer) – Page number (default: 1)

**Response Fields per Commit:**
- `url`, `sha`, `node_id`, `html_url`, `comments_url`
- `commit` object containing:
  - `url`, `author`, `committer`, `message`, `comment_count`
  - `tree` (with `sha` and `url`)
  - `verification` object (see below)
- `author` and `committer` (Simple User objects or null)
- `parents` array (sha, url, html_url)
- `stats` (additions, deletions, total)
- `files` array (Diff Entry objects)

---

### 2. List Branches for HEAD Commit
**GET** `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{commit_sha}/branches-where-head`

Shows all branches where a specific commit SHA is the latest.

**Parameters:**
- `commit_sha` (string, required) – The commit SHA

**Response Fields:**
- `name` (string) – Branch name
- `commit` object (sha, url)
- `protected` (boolean)

---

### 3. List Pull Requests Associated with Commit
**GET** `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{commit_sha}/pulls`

Returns merged and open pull requests linked to a commit.

**Query Parameters:**
- `per_page` (integer) – Max 100 (default: 30)
- `page` (integer) – Page number (default: 1)

**Response:** Array of Pull Request Simple objects including:
- `url`, `id`, `node_id`, `html_url`, `diff_url`, `patch_url`
- `issue_url`, `commits_url`, `review_comments_url`, `comments_url`, `statuses_url`
- `number`, `state`, `locked`, `title`, `user`, `body`, `labels`
- `milestone`, `active_lock_reason`
- `created_at`, `updated_at`, `closed_at`, `merged_at`
- `assignees`, `requested_reviewers`, `requested_teams`
- `head` and `base` objects (with label, ref, repo, sha, user)
- `_links` (comments, commits, statuses, html, issue, review_comments, self)
- `author_association`, `auto_merge`, `draft`

---

### 4. Get a Single Commit
**GET** `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}`

Retrieves a specific commit by SHA, branch name, or tag.

**Query Parameters:**
- `page` (integer) – Page number (default: 1)
- `per_page` (integer) – Max 100 (default: 30)

**Response:** Commit object (same structure as List Commits)

**Media Type Options:**
- `application/vnd.github.diff` – Returns diff
- `application/vnd.github.patch` – Returns patch
- `application/vnd.github.sha` – Returns SHA-1 hash only

---

### 5. Compare Two Commits
**GET** `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/compare/{basehead}`

Compares commits between two refs. Format: `BASE...HEAD` or for different repos: `USERNAME:BASE...USERNAME:HEAD`

**Query Parameters:**
- `page` (integer) – Page number (default: 1)
- `per_page` (integer) – Max 100 (default: 30)

**Response Fields:**
- `url`, `html_url`, `permalink_url`, `diff_url`, `patch_url`
- `base_commit` and `merge_base_commit` (Commit objects)
- `status` (enum: diverged, ahead, behind, identical)
- `ahead_by`, `behind_by`, `total_commits`
- `commits` array (Commit objects)
- `files` array (Diff Entry objects)

---

## Verification Object (included in commit responses)

- `verified` (boolean) – Whether GitHub verified the signature
- `reason` (string) – Verification result (see reason values below)
- `signature` (string or null) – Extracted signature
- `payload` (string or null) – Signed value
- `verified_at` (string or null) – Verification timestamp

**Reason Values:**
`expired_key`, `not_signing_key`, `gpgverify_error`, `gpgverify_unavailable`, `unsigned`, `unknown_signature_type`, `no_user`, `unverified_email`, `bad_email`, `unknown_key`, `malformed_signature`, `invalid`, `valid`

---

## Diff Entry Object (for file changes)

- `sha` (string or null)
- `filename` (string)
- `status` (enum: added, removed, modified, renamed, copied, changed, unchanged)
- `additions`, `deletions`, `changes` (integers)
- `blob_url`, `raw_url`, `contents_url` (URIs)
- `patch` (string, optional)
- `previous_filename` (string, optional)

---

## HTTP Status Codes

- **200** – Success
- **400** – Bad Request
- **404** – Not Found
- **409** – Conflict
- **422** – Validation failed or endpoint spammed
- **500** – Internal Error
- **503** – Service Unavailable

# GitHub Repository Traffic API Endpoints

## All Traffic Endpoints

**1. Get Repository Clones**
- Endpoint: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/traffic/clones`
- Returns total clones and daily/weekly breakdown for 14 days
- Fields: `count`, `uniques`, `clones` (array with `timestamp`, `uniques`, `count`)

**2. Get Page Views**
- Endpoint: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/traffic/views`
- Returns total views and daily/weekly breakdown for 14 days
- Fields: `count`, `uniques`, `views` (array with `timestamp`, `uniques`, `count`)

**3. Get Top Referral Paths**
- Endpoint: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/traffic/popular/paths`
- Returns top 10 popular contents from last 14 days
- Fields: `path`, `title`, `count`, `uniques`

**4. Get Top Referral Sources**
- Endpoint: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/traffic/popular/referrers`
- Returns top 10 referrers from last 14 days
- Fields: `referrer`, `count`, `uniques`

## Common Requirements
All endpoints require write access to the repository and return HTTP 200 (success) or 403 (forbidden) status codes. The `per` parameter (day/week) is available for clones and views endpoints only.

# GitHub Repository Statistics Endpoints

## Endpoints Overview

### 1. **Code Frequency** (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/code_frequency`)
Returns weekly aggregate of additions and deletions. Limited to repositories with fewer than 10,000 commits.
- **Response**: "Array of array"

### 2. **Commit Activity** (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/commit_activity`)
Provides "the last year of commit activity grouped by week."
- **Fields**: 
  - `days` (array of integers)
  - `total` (integer)
  - `week` (integer)

### 3. **Contributors** (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/contributors`)
Shows total commits per contributor with weekly breakdowns.
- **Fields**:
  - `author` (null or Simple User object with login, id, avatar_url, etc.)
  - `total` (integer)
  - `weeks` (array with `w`, `a`, `d`, `c` integers)

### 4. **Participation** (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/participation`)
Displays "total commit counts for the owner and total commit counts in all" over 52 weeks.
- **Fields**:
  - `all` (array of integers)
  - `owner` (array of integers)

### 5. **Punch Card** (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/punch_card`)
Hourly commit distribution by day of week: "[day, hour, commit_count]" format.
- **Response**: Array matching code frequency schema

All endpoints support 200, 202 (caching), and 204 responses; merge commits are excluded from statistics.

# GitHub Actions Workflow Run Fields

Based on the REST API documentation, here are the primary fields available for workflow runs:

## Core Identification
- `id` - Unique workflow run identifier
- `node_id` - GraphQL node identifier
- `name` - Workflow run name
- `run_number` - Sequential run number
- `run_attempt` - Attempt number for reruns

## Execution Status
- `status` - Current state (queued, in_progress, completed, etc.)
- `conclusion` - Final result (success, failure, cancelled, neutral, skipped, etc.)
- `event` - Triggering event type (push, pull_request, issue, etc.)

## Timing Information
- `created_at` - Creation timestamp
- `updated_at` - Last update timestamp
- `run_started_at` - When execution began

## Code Reference
- `head_sha` - Commit SHA triggering the run
- `head_branch` - Branch name
- `path` - Workflow file path
- `workflow_id` - Associated workflow identifier

## Related Resources
- `url`, `html_url` - API and web URLs
- `jobs_url`, `logs_url`, `artifacts_url` - Resource endpoints
- `check_suite_id`, `check_suite_node_id` - Check run associations
- `pull_requests` - Associated PR information (for PR-triggered runs)

## Actor Information
- `actor` - User who initiated the run
- `triggering_actor` - User whose action triggered execution

## Repository Context
- `repository` - Associated repository details
- `head_repository`, `head_repository_id` - Source repository info

## Deployment & Review
- `cancel_url`, `rerun_url` - Action endpoints
- `previous_attempt_url` - Link to prior attempt

# Workflow Job Fields

Based on the GitHub REST API documentation, here are all fields available for workflow jobs:

**Core Identifiers:**
- `id`, `run_id`, `node_id`, `job_id`

**Timing Information:**
- `created_at`, `started_at`, `completed_at` (all date-time format)

**Status & Results:**
- `status` (queued, in_progress, completed, waiting, requested, pending)
- `conclusion` (success, failure, neutral, cancelled, skipped, timed_out, action_required)

**Job Details:**
- `name`, `head_sha`, `head_branch`, `workflow_name`
- `run_url`, `url`, `html_url`, `check_run_url`

**Runner Information:**
- `runner_id`, `runner_name`, `runner_group_id`, `runner_group_name`

**Configuration:**
- `labels` (array of strings)
- `run_attempt`

**Steps:**
Each job contains a `steps` array with objects containing:
- `name`, `number`, `status`, `conclusion`
- `started_at`, `completed_at`

This comprehensive schema enables tracking of "job duration through started_at and completed_at timestamps" plus full step-level execution details and runner assignment information.

# GitHub Release Fields

Based on the REST API documentation, here are the available fields for releases:

**Core Release Information:**
- `tag_name` - The name of the Git tag
- `target_commitish` - The commit or branch the tag references
- `name` - The release name
- `body` - Text describing the release contents
- `id` - Unique release identifier
- `node_id` - GraphQL node identifier

**Release Status:**
- `draft` - Boolean indicating unpublished status
- `prerelease` - Boolean identifying pre-release versions
- `published_at` - Publication timestamp
- `created_at` - Creation timestamp
- `updated_at` - Last modification timestamp

**Author & Reactions:**
- `author` - Simple User object with profile information
- `reactions` - Reaction Rollup with counts for +1, -1, laugh, confused, heart, hooray, eyes, and rocket

**Assets & URLs:**
- `assets` - Array of Release Asset objects
- `url` - API endpoint URI
- `html_url` - Web interface URL
- `assets_url` - Assets endpoint
- `upload_url` - Hypermedia resource for uploads
- `tarball_url` and `zipball_url` - Archive download links

**Additional Fields:**
- `body_html` and `body_text` - Formatted release descriptions
- `mentions_count` - User mentions in release content
- `discussion_url` - Linked discussion page

# Milestone Fields in GitHub REST API

Based on the documentation, here are the available milestone fields:

**Core Fields:**
- `url` - URI reference
- `html_url` - Web interface link
- `labels_url` - Associated labels endpoint
- `id` - Numeric identifier
- `node_id` - GraphQL node identifier
- `number` - Milestone number
- `title` - Milestone name
- `description` - Details about the milestone
- `state` - Current status (`open` or `closed`)
- `due_on` - Target completion date in ISO 8601 format
- `created_at` - Creation timestamp
- `updated_at` - Last modification timestamp
- `closed_at` - Closure timestamp (if applicable)

**Progress Tracking:**
- `open_issues` - Count of unresolved issues
- `closed_issues` - Count of resolved issues

**Creator Information:**
- `creator` - User who created the milestone (includes login, avatar_url, and other user details)

Note: The documentation doesn't explicitly list a "progress" field. You can calculate progress using the ratio of `closed_issues` to total issues (`closed_issues + open_issues`).

# Repository Object Fields

Based on the GitHub REST API documentation, here are the key fields available in a repository object:

## Basic Information
- `id`, `node_id`, `name`, `full_name`
- `owner` (Simple User object)
- `private`, `visibility`
- `description`, `homepage`
- `html_url`, `url`

## Repository Statistics
- `size` - repository size in bytes
- `stargazers_count` - number of stars
- `forks_count` - number of forks
- `watchers_count` - number of watchers
- `open_issues_count` - number of open issues
- `forks`, `open_issues`, `watchers` (alternate fields)

## Configuration Flags
- `has_issues` - "Either true to enable issues for this repository or false to disable them"
- `has_projects`, `has_wiki`, `has_pages`, `has_discussions`, `has_pull_requests`
- `is_template` - whether repo serves as a template
- `archived`, `disabled`
- `allow_forking`, `web_commit_signoff_required`

## Merge Strategy Settings
- `allow_merge_commit` - enables standard merge commits
- `allow_squash_merge` - permits squash-merge operations
- `allow_rebase_merge` - permits rebase-merge operations
- `allow_auto_merge` - enables automatic merging
- `delete_branch_on_merge` - auto-deletes head branches after merging
- `allow_update_branch` - allows updating behind branches

## Merge Commit Defaults
- `merge_commit_title`, `merge_commit_message`
- `squash_merge_commit_title`, `squash_merge_commit_message`

## Metadata
- `default_branch`
- `language` - primary programming language
- `topics` - array of topic tags
- `created_at`, `updated_at`, `pushed_at`

## Security & Analysis
- `security_and_analysis` object containing status for advanced security, secret scanning, and dependency features

# GitHub User Profile Fields

Based on the REST API documentation, here are the fields available in a GitHub user/contributor profile:

**Basic Identity:**
- login, id, node_id, type, site_admin

**Profile Information:**
- name, company, blog, location, email, notification_email, hireable, bio, twitter_username

**Activity Metrics:**
- public_repos, public_gists, followers, following, created_at, updated_at

**Additional Fields (Private Users Only):**
- private_gists, total_private_repos, owned_private_repos, disk_usage, collaborators, two_factor_authentication

**URLs:**
- avatar_url, gravatar_id, url, html_url, followers_url, following_url, gists_url, starred_url, subscriptions_url, organizations_url, repos_url, events_url, received_events_url

**Plan Information:**
- plan (object containing: name, space, private_repos, collaborators)

**Additional Optional Fields:**
- user_view_type, business_plus, ldap_dn, starred_at

The response varies depending on authentication level—private user profiles contain additional fields like "disk_usage" and "two_factor_authentication," while public profiles exclude sensitive account details.

# GitHub Events API: Available Event Types

Based on the documentation, GitHub tracks developer activity through these event types:

## Core Event Types

**Repository Activity:**
- **PushEvent** - Tracks commits pushed to repositories
- **CreateEvent** - Monitors branch and tag creation
- **DeleteEvent** - Records branch and tag deletion
- **PublicEvent** - Marks when repositories become public

**Collaboration Events:**
- **PullRequestEvent** - Captures PR creation and updates
- **PullRequestReviewEvent** - Logs code reviews
- **PullRequestReviewCommentEvent** - Tracks review comments
- **IssuesEvent** - Records issue creation and modifications
- **IssueCommentEvent** - Documents issue discussion activity

**Content Management:**
- **CommitCommentEvent** - Monitors comments on commits
- **GollumEvent** - Tracks wiki page changes
- **ReleaseEvent** - Records software releases

**Social & Administrative:**
- **WatchEvent** - Logs repository stars/watches
- **ForkEvent** - Tracks repository forks
- **MemberEvent** - Records collaborator additions
- **DiscussionEvent** - Captures discussion interactions

## Key Constraint

The API documentation notes that "the timeline will include up to 300 events. Only events created within the past 30 days will be included," making it suitable for recent activity monitoring rather than historical analysis.

# Check Run Fields Summary

Here are the key fields available for check runs in the GitHub REST API:

## Core Identification
- `id` (integer): Unique identifier
- `node_id` (string): GraphQL node identifier
- `head_sha` (string): Commit SHA the check run applies to
- `external_id` (string or null): Reference on the integrator's system

## Status & Conclusion
- `status`: Current state - "queued", "in_progress", "completed", "waiting", "requested", or "pending"
- `conclusion`: Final result - "success", "failure", "neutral", "cancelled", "skipped", "timed_out", "action_required", or null

## Timing
- `started_at` (date-time or null): When the check began
- `completed_at` (date-time or null): When the check finished

## Output Information
The `output` object contains:
- `title` (string or null): Report heading
- `summary` (string or null): Overview supporting Markdown
- `text` (string or null): Detailed description with Markdown support
- `annotations_count` (integer): Total annotations attached
- `annotations_url` (URI): Link to annotation list

## Additional Fields
- `name` (string): Check identifier (e.g., "code-coverage")
- `url` (string): API endpoint for this check run
- `html_url` (string or null): GitHub UI location
- `details_url` (string or null): Integration site with full details
- `check_suite` (object): Parent suite information
- `app` (GitHub app object or null): Creating application details
- `pull_requests` (array): Associated pull requests
- `deployment` (object): Related deployment info

# Code Scanning REST API Endpoints & Alert Fields

## Key Endpoints

**Organization-level alerts:**
- `GET /orgs/{org}/code-scanning/alerts` - List alerts across eligible organization repositories

**Repository-level alerts:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/code-scanning/alerts` - List all repository alerts

**Assignment & repository context:**

**Filtering parameters available:**
- `tool_name` / `tool_guid` - Filter by scanner
- `state`, `severity` - Filter by status/impact

# ContributionsCollection GraphQL Object Fields

Based on the GraphQL schema documentation, here are the fields in the `ContributionsCollection` object:

## Core Contribution Fields

- `commitContributionsByRepository`: "Commit contributions made by the user, grouped by repository"
- `pullRequestContributionsByRepository`: Pull request contributions organized by repository
- `issueContributionsByRepository`: Issue contributions organized by repository
- `repositoryContributions`: Repositories created by the user within the time range
- `pullRequestReviewContributions`: Pull request reviews submitted by the user
- `pullRequestReviewContributionsByRepository`: Reviews grouped by repository

## Summary Statistics

- `totalCommitContributions`: Count of commits made by the user
- `totalPullRequestContributions`: Number of pull requests opened
- `totalIssueContributions`: Count of issues opened
- `totalPullRequestReviewContributions`: Number of reviews left
- `totalRepositoryContributions`: Repositories created by the user
- `totalRepositoriesWithContributedCommits`: "How many different repositories the user committed to"
- `totalRepositoriesWithContributedIssues`: Distinct repositories where issues were opened
- `totalRepositoriesWithContributedPullRequests`: Repositories receiving pull requests
- `totalRepositoriesWithContributedPullRequestReviews`: Repositories where reviews were submitted
- `restrictedContributionsCount`: "Count of contributions made by the user that the viewer cannot access"

## Calendar & Timeline

- `contributionCalendar`: Visual representation of contributions over time
- `contributionYears`: Years when user made contributions

## Additional Fields

- `user`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `earliestRestrictedContributionDate`, `latestRestrictedContributionDate`, `hasActivityInThePast`, `hasAnyContributions`, `hasAnyRestrictedContributions`, `isSingleDay`

# GitHub Repository Contents API Endpoints

## Available Endpoints

The documentation covers these main endpoints:

1. **GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}** - Retrieve file or directory contents
2. **PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}** - Create or update files
3. **DELETE /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}** - Delete files
4. **GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/readme** - Get repository README
5. **GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/readme/{dir}** - Get README from a directory
6. **GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/tarball/{ref}** - Download tar archive
7. **GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/zipball/{ref}** - Download zip archive

## File Metadata Available

Yes, the GET endpoint returns comprehensive metadata including:
- **SHA** (blob identifier)
- **File size** (in bytes)
- **Encoding** (e.g., base64)
- **Content** (Base64 encoded for files)
- **Type** (file, directory, symlink, or submodule)
- **URLs** (download, git, HTML)

## Git Trees API for Full Listings

The documentation mentions: "To get a repository's contents recursively, you can recursively get the tree." However, it notes there's a "1,000 file upper limit for a directory" and directs users to "use the Git Trees API" for retrieving more files. The actual Git Trees API endpoints aren't detailed in this documentation.

# Git Commit Fields via Low-Level API

Based on the GitHub REST API documentation, the following fields are available for git commits:

**Core Identifiers:**
- `sha` - Commit hash
- `node_id` - GraphQL node identifier
- `url` - API endpoint URI

**Content:**
- `message` - "The commit message"
- `tree` - Object containing the tree SHA and URL

**Authorship:**
- `author` - Object with name, email, and ISO 8601 date
- `committer` - Object with name, email, and ISO 8601 date (defaults to author if omitted)

**Relationships:**
- `parents` - Array of parent commit SHAs and URLs

**Signature Verification:**
The verification object includes:
- `verified` - Boolean indicating GitHub's verification result
- `reason` - Status explanation (e.g., "valid", "unsigned", "invalid")
- `signature` - Extracted PGP signature or null
- `payload` - Signed value or null
- `verified_at` - Verification timestamp or null

**Display:**
- `html_url` - Web interface link

When creating commits, you must provide `message`, `tree`, and optionally `parents`, `author`, `committer`, and `signature`.

# GitHub API Rate Limits Summary

## Primary Rate Limits for Authenticated Users

**Authenticated users** have "a personal rate limit of 5,000 requests per hour." Enterprise Cloud organizations grant "a higher rate limit of 15,000 requests per hour."

**GitHub Apps** using installation access tokens receive "the installation's minimum rate limit of 5,000 requests per hour," scaling up to 12,500 for larger installations, or 15,000 for Enterprise Cloud.

**OAuth Apps** follow the same rules as authenticated users: 5,000 requests per hour base, 15,000 for Enterprise Cloud organizations.

## Secondary Rate Limits

GitHub enforces additional restrictions preventing abuse:
- Maximum 100 concurrent requests
- No more than 900 points per minute on REST endpoints
- Maximum 90 seconds CPU time per 60 seconds real time
- No more than 80 content-generating requests per minute
- Maximum 2,000 OAuth token requests per hour

## Response Headers

GitHub returns these rate limit indicators with each response:

| Header | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `x-ratelimit-limit` | Maximum requests per hour allowed |
| `x-ratelimit-remaining` | Requests left in current window |
| `x-ratelimit-used` | Requests consumed this window |
| `x-ratelimit-reset` | Window reset time (UTC epoch seconds) |
| `x-ratelimit-resource` | Which rate limit bucket was consumed |

## GraphQL API Rate Limit

The document states "The GraphQL API also has a separate primary rate limit" but provides no specific numbers for GraphQL's primary limit—only secondary limits of 2,000 points per minute.

# GraphQL API Rate Limits

## Primary Rate Limits (Points per Hour)

- **Individual users**: "5,000 points per hour per user" via personal access tokens, GitHub Apps, or OAuth apps
- **Enterprise Cloud users**: 10,000 points per hour when using apps owned by their organization
- **GitHub App installations (non-Enterprise)**: "5,000 points per hour per installation" with bonuses of 50 points per hour for repos/users exceeding 20
- **GitHub App installations (Enterprise Cloud)**: "10,000 points per hour per installation"
- **GitHub Actions**: "1,000 points per hour per repository" (15,000 for enterprise resources)

## Secondary Rate Limits (Points per Minute)

The API enforces "no more than 2,000 points per minute" for the GraphQL endpoint. Individual request costs for secondary limits are:
- Queries without mutations: 1 point
- Queries with mutations: 5 points

## Node Limits

GraphQL calls cannot exceed "500,000 total nodes" per request. The `first` or `last` arguments must fall "within 1-100" range.

## Additional Constraints

- Maximum 100 concurrent requests allowed
- 10-second timeout window for request processing
- Minimum point value per call: 1 point

# GitHub REST API Search Endpoints

## Searchable Items

The GitHub REST API supports searching across six primary categories:

1. **Code** - Search within file contents
2. **Commits** - Find commits via various criteria on default branches
3. **Issues and Pull Requests** - Locate issues and PRs by state and keywords
4. **Labels** - Search label names and descriptions
5. **Repositories** - Find repos by various criteria
6. **Topics** - Discover topics by keywords
7. **Users** - Search for users by various criteria

## Key Qualifiers for Developer Performance Tracking

### For Code Search:
- `language:` - Filter by programming language
- `in:file` - Search within file contents
- `repo:` - Limit to specific repositories
- File size restrictions apply (max 384 KB)

### For Commit Search:
- `repo:` - Target specific repositories
- `author-date` and `committer-date` - Sort by temporal data
- Default branch searching only

Both endpoints support "up to 100 results per page" and require authentication for optimal rate limits (9-30 requests per minute depending on endpoint).

# GitHub Collaborators API Endpoints

## Listing Endpoints

**List all collaborators:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/collaborators` - Returns array of collaborators with their permissions and roles

**Check individual collaborator:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/collaborators/{username}` - Verifies if a user is a collaborator (returns 204 if yes, 404 if no)

**Get user's permission level:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/collaborators/{username}/permission` - Shows the specific permission and role assigned

## Available Permission Levels

The API supports five distinct permission tiers:

1. **pull** - Read-only access
2. **triage** - Can manage issues and pull requests
3. **push** - Can push code changes
4. **maintain** - Can manage repository settings
5. **admin** - Full administrative access

Additionally, repositories can define "custom repository role names" beyond these standard levels. The documentation notes that the `role_name` field represents "the highest role assigned to the collaborator after considering all sources of grants, including: repo, teams, organization, and enterprise."

# CommitContributionsByRepository Fields

Based on the GraphQL schema provided, **CommitContributionsByRepository** exposes the following fields for accessing contribution data per repository:

1. **contributions** - A connection to `CreatedCommitContributionConnection` showing "The commit contributions, each representing a day" with pagination support and ordering options

2. **repository** - Returns the `Repository` object for "The repository in which the commits were made"

3. **resourcePath** - Provides "The HTTP path for the user's commits to the repository in this time range"

4. **url** - Supplies "The HTTP URL for the user's commits to the repository in this time range"

**Summary**: This object aggregates commit-level contribution metrics at the repository level, allowing you to retrieve daily commit counts, access the repository details, and get both resource and web URLs for a user's commits within a specific repository during a defined time period.

# GitHub Actions Cache Usage Data

## Available Endpoints & Fields

**Organization-Level Usage:**
- `GET /orgs/{org}/actions/cache/usage` - Returns `total_active_caches_count` and `total_active_caches_size_in_bytes`
- `GET /orgs/{org}/actions/cache/usage-by-repository` - Lists repos with `active_caches_count`, `active_caches_size_in_bytes`, and `full_name`

**Repository-Level Usage:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/cache/usage` - Provides `active_caches_count`, `active_caches_size_in_bytes`, and `full_name`

**Cache Details:**
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/caches` - Lists individual caches with:
  - `id`, `key`, `ref`, `version`
  - `created_at` (date-time format)
  - `last_accessed_at` (date-time format)
  - `size_in_bytes`

## Important Note

The documentation does not include "cache hits" metrics. Available size metrics are expressed in bytes or gigabytes depending on the endpoint. The API data refreshes approximately every 5 minutes, so reported values may lag actual cache operations.

# GitHub Projects API Overview

The documentation provided focuses on **Projects v2** REST API endpoints, not classic Projects. Here's what's covered:

## Projects v2 Endpoints

The page documents four main endpoints:

1. **List organization projects**: `GET /orgs/{org}/projectsV2`
2. **Get specific organization project**: `GET /orgs/{org}/projectsV2/{project_number}`
3. **List user projects**: `GET /users/{username}/projectsV2`
4. **Get specific user project**: `GET /users/{username}/projectsV2/{project_number}`

## Key Project Fields Returned

Response objects include:
- `id`, `node_id`, `number`
- `title`, `description`, `short_description`
- `public` (boolean), `state` (open/closed)
- `owner` and `creator` (Simple User objects)
- `created_at`, `updated_at`, `closed_at`, `deleted_at`
- `is_template` (boolean)
- `latest_status_update` with status options: INACTIVE, ON_TRACK, AT_RISK, OFF_TRACK, COMPLETE

## Notable Limitation

The provided content contains no information about **classic Projects** or their columns/cards structure. The documentation exclusively covers Projects v2, which appears to be GitHub's current project management solution with a different data model than the classic version.

# GitHub Commit Status Fields and Endpoints

## Core Commit Status Fields

**state** - Required string indicating status outcome: `error`, `failure`, `pending`, or `success`

**description** - Optional string or null providing a brief summary of the status

**context** - Required string label (case-insensitive) differentiating this status from others; defaults to `default`

**target_url** - Optional URI linking to relevant output (e.g., build logs)

**creator** - Required field containing either null or Simple User object with account details

Additional fields include: `url`, `avatar_url`, `id`, `node_id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`

## REST API Endpoints

**1. Get Combined Status**
```
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}/status
```
Retrieves merged status view for a given SHA, branch, or tag. Returns overall state and individual statuses.

**2. List Commit Statuses**
```
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}/statuses
```
Displays all statuses for a reference in reverse chronological order.

**3. Create Commit Status**
```
POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/statuses/{sha}
```
Creates new status for a commit SHA. Note: "there is a limit of 1000 statuses per sha and context" within a repository.

# List Commits Endpoint: Query Parameters and Response Fields

## Query Parameters

The "List commits" endpoint (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits`) accepts these query parameters:

- **`sha`** (string): "SHA or branch to start listing commits from. Default: the repository's default branch (usually main)."
- **`path`** (string): "Only commits containing this file path will be returned."
- **`author`** (string): "GitHub username or email address to use to filter by commit author."
- **`committer`** (string): "GitHub username or email address to use to filter by commit committer."
- **`since`** (string): Timestamp in ISO 8601 format filtering results updated after given time.
- **`until`** (string): Timestamp in ISO 8601 format limiting results to commits before specified date.
- **`per_page`** (integer): "The number of results per page (max 100)." Default: 30
- **`page`** (integer): "The page number of the results to fetch." Default: 1

## Stats Field in List Response

**The `stats` object is NOT included in the list commits response.** The response schema shows `stats` as an optional field (not marked required) containing additions, deletions, and total counts. However, documentation indicates these detailed stats appear only in the "Get a commit" endpoint for individual commit details, not when listing multiple commits.

# CreatedCommitContribution Fields

Based on the GraphQL schema, here are all the fields for the `CreatedCommitContribution` object:

1. **commitCount** (Int!): "How many commits were made on this day to this repository by the user."

2. **isRestricted** (Boolean!): Indicates whether the contribution is associated with a record lacking access permissions.

3. **occurredAt** (DateTime!): "When this contribution was made."

4. **repository** (Repository!): The repository where the commit was made.

5. **resourcePath** (URI!): "The HTTP path for this contribution."

6. **url** (URI!): "The HTTP URL for this contribution."

7. **user** (User!): "The user who made this contribution."

The `CreatedCommitContribution` type implements the `Contribution` interface and represents a user's contribution through commits to a repository.

# Timeline Events for Issues

Based on the documentation, the following timeline event types are available for issues and pull requests:

## Event Types

1. **Labeled Issue Event** - Triggered when a label is added
2. **Unlabeled Issue Event** - Triggered when a label is removed
3. **Milestoned Issue Event** - Triggered when a milestone is assigned
4. **Demilestoned Issue Event** - Triggered when a milestone is removed
5. **Renamed Issue Event** - Triggered when issue title changes
6. **Review Requested Issue Event** - Triggered when a reviewer is requested (pull requests)
7. **Review Request Removed Issue Event** - Triggered when a review request is withdrawn
8. **Review Dismissed Issue Event** - Triggered when a review is dismissed
9. **Locked Issue Event** - Triggered when an issue is locked
10. **Added to Project Issue Event** - Triggered when added to a project
11. **Moved Column in Project Issue Event** - Triggered when moved within a project
12. **Removed from Project Issue Event** - Triggered when removed from a project
13. **Converted Note to Issue Event** - Triggered when a note becomes an issue
14. **Timeline Comment Event** - Issue or PR comments
15. **Timeline Cross Referenced Event** - Cross-references between issues
16. **Timeline Committed Event** - Commit information
17. **Timeline Reviewed Event** - Pull request reviews
18. **Timeline Line Commented Event** - Comments on specific code lines
19. **Timeline Commit Commented Event** - Comments on commits
20. **Timeline Assigned Issue Event** - Assignee added
21. **Timeline Unassigned Issue Event** - Assignee removed
22. **State Change Issue Event** - Open/closed status changes

Each event includes fields for actor, timestamp, and event-specific metadata.

content.content:
Based on the documentation provided, there is **no language breakdown endpoint** listed under repository contents.

The available endpoints for repository contents are:

1. **Get repository content** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}`
2. **Create or update file contents** - `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}`
3. **Delete a file** - `DELETE /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}`
4. **Get a repository README** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/readme`
5. **Get a repository README for a directory** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/readme/{dir}`
6. **Download repository archive (tar)** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/tarball/{ref}`
7. **Download repository archive (zip)** - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/zipball/{ref}`

None of these endpoints provide programming language statistics or breakdowns. The documentation does not mention a dedicated "languages" endpoint.

# List Repository Languages Endpoint

The endpoint is:
```
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/languages
```

## Return Format

According to the documentation, it returns:

**Response schema (Status: 200):**
"object, additional properties: integer"

In other words, the endpoint returns a JSON object where each key represents a programming language name, and each value is an integer representing the number of bytes of code written in that language.

**Example structure:**
```json
{
  "Python": 432891,
  "JavaScript": 234567,
  "TypeScript": 123456
}
```

The documentation notes that "The value shown for each language is the number of bytes of code written in that language."

# PullRequestReviewDecision and Related Pull Request Enums

## PullRequestReviewDecision

The review status of a pull request, with these values:

- **APPROVED**: "The pull request has received an approving review."
- **CHANGES_REQUESTED**: "Changes have been requested on the pull request."
- **REVIEW_REQUIRED**: "A review is required before the pull request can be merged."

## Related Pull Request State Enums

### PullRequestState
Represents the possible states of a pull request:

- **OPEN**: "A pull request that is still open."
- **CLOSED**: "A pull request that has been closed without being merged."
- **MERGED**: "A pull request that has been closed by being merged."

### PullRequestUpdateState
The possible target states when updating a pull request:

- **OPEN**: "A pull request that is still open."
- **CLOSED**: "A pull request that has been closed without being merged."

### MergeStateStatus
Detailed status information about a pull request merge, including states like `CLEAN` (mergeable and passing), `BLOCKED`, and `DRAFT`.

These enums work together to track both the review approval status and the overall lifecycle state of pull requests in the GraphQL API.

---

# GitHub API Data Points for Developer Performance Tracking: Comprehensive Reference

---

## 1. COMMITS

### REST Endpoints
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits` — List commits (filterable by `sha`, `path`, `author`, `committer`, `since`, `until`)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}` — Get single commit (full detail)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{commit_sha}/pulls` — PRs associated with a commit
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{commit_sha}/branches-where-head` — Branches where this commit is HEAD
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/compare/{basehead}` — Compare two refs (`ahead_by`, `behind_by`, `total_commits`, `status`)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}/statuses` — CI statuses on a commit
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{ref}/status` — Combined status (aggregate pass/fail)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/commits/{commit_sha}` — Low-level git commit object

### Commit Object Fields
- `sha`, `node_id`, `html_url`
- `commit.message` — full message text (subject + body)
- `commit.author.name`, `.email`, `.date` — **authored timestamp** (different from committed timestamp)
- `commit.committer.name`, `.email`, `.date` — **committed timestamp**
- `commit.comment_count` — number of comments on this commit
- `commit.tree.sha` — tree SHA
- `commit.verification.verified` (boolean), `.reason`, `.signature`, `.payload`, `.verified_at` — GPG/SSH signature verification
- `author` (Simple User or null) — GitHub account if matched by email
- `committer` (Simple User or null)
- `parents` array — SHAs of parent commits (multiple = merge commit)
- **`stats.additions`**, **`stats.deletions`**, **`stats.total`** — ONLY on single-commit endpoint, NOT on list
- `files[]` — array of Diff Entry objects per file:
  - `filename`, `previous_filename` (on rename)
  - `status`: `added`, `removed`, `modified`, `renamed`, `copied`, `changed`, `unchanged`
  - `additions`, `deletions`, `changes`
  - `patch` — unified diff text
  - `blob_url`, `raw_url`, `contents_url`

### Compare Endpoint Fields
- `status`: `diverged`, `ahead`, `behind`, `identical`
- `ahead_by`, `behind_by`, `total_commits`
- `base_commit`, `merge_base_commit`
- `commits[]`, `files[]`

### Important Notes
- Stats (additions/deletions) require N+1 calls — one per commit — when processing a list. Budget accordingly.
- Merge commits have 2+ parents; you can detect merges by `parents.length > 1`.
- `author.date` vs `committer.date` differ when commits are rebased or cherry-picked.
- List endpoint max: 100 per page; no server-side aggregation.

### Performance-Relevant Derived Metrics
- **Time to first review** = `review.submitted_at` − `pr.created_at`
- **Review turnaround** per reviewer from `review.submitted_at`
- **Review cycles** = count of CHANGES_REQUESTED → push → re-review sequences
- **Thread resolution rate** = resolved threads / total threads
- **Comment-to-approval ratio** = `review_comments` / number of APPROVED reviews
- **Reviewer responsiveness** = time between `review_requested_at` (from timeline) and `review.submitted_at`

## 5. REPOSITORY STATISTICS & TRAFFIC

### Statistics Endpoints (cached, async — may return 202 while computing)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/contributors`
  - Per contributor: `author` (Simple User), `total` (all-time commits), `weeks[]` with `w` (Unix timestamp), `a` (additions), `d` (deletions), `c` (commits)
  - **Note**: excludes merge commits

- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/commit_activity`
  - Last 52 weeks: `week` (Unix timestamp), `total`, `days[]` (Sun–Sat array of commit counts)

- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/code_frequency`
  - Weekly additions/deletions since repo creation: `[week_unix_timestamp, additions, deletions]`
  - **Note**: limited to repos with <10,000 commits

- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/participation`
  - Last 52 weeks: `all[]` (total commits), `owner[]` (commits by repo owner)
  - Useful for owner vs. community contribution split

- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/stats/punch_card`
  - Hourly distribution: `[day_of_week (0=Sun), hour (0–23), commit_count]`
  - Great for visualizing work patterns / time zone analysis

### Traffic Endpoints (require push access, 14-day window only)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/traffic/views` — `count` (total), `uniques`, `views[]` with `timestamp`, `count`, `uniques`

- Fields: `number`, `state` (`open`, `resolved`), `secret_type`, `secret_type_display_name`, `provider`, `validity` (`active`, `inactive`, `unknown`), `resolution` (`false_positive`, `wont_fix`, `revoked`, `used_in_tests`), `resolved_at`, `resolved_by`, `push_protection_bypassed` (boolean), `push_protection_bypassed_by`, `push_protection_bypassed_at`, `publicly_leaked`, `multi_repo`, `created_at`, `updated_at`

### Dependabot Alerts
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/dependabot/alerts`
- `GET /orgs/{org}/dependabot/alerts`
- Fields: `number`, `state` (`auto_dismissed`, `dismissed`, `fixed`, `open`), `severity` (`low`, `medium`, `high`, `critical`), `package.ecosystem`, `package.name`, `vulnerable_version_range`, `first_patched_version`, `dismissed_reason` (`fix_started`, `inaccurate`, `no_bandwidth`, `not_used`, `tolerable_risk`), `dismissed_by`, `dismissed_at`, `auto_dismissed_at`, `fixed_at`, `dependency.scope` (`development`/`runtime`), `security_advisory.ghsa_id`, `security_advisory.cve_id`, `security_advisory.severity`, `assignees[]`

### Dependency Review (per PR/comparison)
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/dependency-graph/compare/{basehead}`
- Fields per dependency: `change_type` (`added`/`removed`), `manifest`, `ecosystem`, `name`, `version`, `scope`, `vulnerabilities[]` (each with `severity`, `advisory_ghsa_id`, `advisory_summary`, `advisory_url`)

## 12. DEPLOYMENTS & ENVIRONMENTS

### Deployments
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` — filter by `sha`, `ref`, `task`, `environment`
- Fields: `id`, `sha`, `ref`, `task` (e.g., `deploy`, `deploy:migrations`), `environment`, `original_environment`, `description`, `creator`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `transient_environment`, `production_environment`, `payload`

### Deployment Statuses
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments/{deployment_id}/statuses`
- Fields: `state` (`error`, `failure`, `pending`, `in_progress`, `queued`, `success`), `description`, `environment_url`, `log_url`, `creator`, `created_at`, `updated_at`

### Environments
- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments` — Lists all environments
- Fields: `name`, `protection_rules[]` (required reviewers, wait timers, branch policies), `deployment_branch_policy`

## 13. EVENTS API (Activity Stream)

- `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/events` — Repo events (last 300, 30 days max)
- `GET /users/{username}/events` — User's public events
- `GET /orgs/{org}/events` — Org events

**Event Types relevant to performance tracking:**
- `PushEvent` — `payload.commits[]` with `sha`, `message`, `author`, `distinct`; `payload.size` (commit count)
- `PullRequestEvent` — action: `opened`, `closed`, `merged`, `edited`, `review_requested`, `ready_for_review`, `converted_to_draft`
- `PullRequestReviewEvent` — `payload.review.state`, `payload.review.submitted_at`
- `PullRequestReviewCommentEvent`
- `IssuesEvent` — action: `opened`, `closed`, `reopened`, `assigned`, `labeled`
- `IssueCommentEvent`
- `CreateEvent` — branch/tag creation: `payload.ref_type`, `payload.ref`
- `DeleteEvent` — branch/tag deletion
- `ReleaseEvent` — `payload.action: published`
- `CommitCommentEvent`
- `ForkEvent` — `payload.forkee`
- `WatchEvent` — star added
- `MemberEvent` — collaborator added
- `GollumEvent` — wiki changes
- `DiscussionEvent`

**Important limitation**: max 300 events, 30-day window. For historical analysis you must use the dedicated endpoints.

## 15. SEARCH API

### Endpoints
- `GET /search/commits` — Search commits (default branch only)
- `GET /search/issues` — Search issues AND pull requests
- `GET /search/code` — Search code/file contents
- `GET /search/repositories`
- `GET /search/users`
- `GET /search/labels`
- `GET /search/topics`

### Useful Qualifiers for Performance Tracking

**Commit search:**
- `author:username`, `committer:username`
- `author-date:>2024-01-01`
- `repo:owner/name`
- `merge:false` — exclude merge commits

**Issue/PR search:**
- `is:pr is:merged merged:2024-01-01..2024-12-31`
- `author:username`, `assignee:username`, `reviewed-by:username`
- `review:approved`, `review:changes_requested`, `review:none`
- `involves:username`
- `label:bug`, `milestone:v1.0`
- `no:assignee`, `no:milestone`
- `comments:>5`
- `reactions:>10`

**Code search:**
- `language:typescript`
- `repo:owner/name`
- `path:src/` — limit to directory
- `filename:.env` — detect risky files

**Rate limit**: 10 req/min (unauthenticated), 30 req/min (authenticated). Max 1,000 results per query.

## 16. RATE LIMITING

### REST API Limits
| User Type | Requests/Hour | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Authenticated user (PAT) | 5,000 | |
| GitHub App installation | 5,000 min, scales to 12,500 | +50/hr per repo >20 |
| Enterprise Cloud user | 15,000 | |
| GitHub App (Enterprise Cloud) | 15,000 | |
| Unauthenticated | 60 | By IP |

**Secondary rate limits (per-minute caps):**
- Max 100 concurrent requests
- Max 900 points/minute on REST
- Max 90s CPU per 60s real-time
- Max 80 content-generating requests/minute
- Max 2,000 OAuth token requests/hour

**Response headers:** `x-ratelimit-limit`, `x-ratelimit-remaining`, `x-ratelimit-used`, `x-ratelimit-reset`, `x-ratelimit-resource`

### GraphQL API Limits
| User Type | Points/Hour |
|---|---|
| Individual user | 5,000 |
| Enterprise Cloud user | 10,000 |
| GitHub App installation | 5,000 (scales with +50/hr per entity >20) |
| GitHub Actions (GITHUB_TOKEN) | 1,000/hr per repo |

**Secondary**: Max 2,000 points/minute. Mutations cost 5 points; queries cost 1 point.
**Node limit**: Max 500,000 nodes per request. `first`/`last` must be 1–100.

### Strategic Implications for a Performance Platform
- **Never** call single-commit detail endpoint for every commit in a list — you'll burn rate limit instantly. Use stats endpoints instead.
- Use **GraphQL batching**: fetch PR + reviews + commits + check runs in a single query
- Use **`since`/`until`** filters on commit list to narrow payloads
- **Webhooks** are your best friend for real-time ingestion — no polling cost
- Cache stats endpoints aggressively — they're computed async and cached by GitHub
- For org-wide data: prefer org-level endpoints over looping through repos
- Use **conditional requests** (`If-None-Match` / `If-Modified-Since`) — 304 responses don't count against rate limit

## 17. WEBHOOKS (Event-Driven Alternative to Polling)

Rather than polling, subscribe to webhooks for real-time ingestion:

**Most relevant events for performance tracking:**
- `push` — commit data (includes `commits[]` with sha, message, author, added/removed/modified files)
- `pull_request` — all PR lifecycle events
- `pull_request_review` — review submitted
- `pull_request_review_comment` — inline comment
- `pull_request_review_thread` — thread resolved/unresolved
- `issues` — issue events
- `issue_comment`
- `workflow_run` — CI run completed
- `workflow_job` — job completed (includes `started_at`, `completed_at`, `conclusion`)
- `check_run`, `check_suite`
- `deployment`, `deployment_status`
- `release`
- `milestone`
- `project_v2_item` — project card moves

Webhook payloads mirror the REST response fields above.

## KEY FIELDS BY USE CASE (Quick Reference)

**Cycle time / Lead time:**
- PR `created_at` → `merged_at` (time in review)
- First commit date in branch → PR `merged_at` (full cycle)
- Issue `created_at` → closing commit date (issue resolution time)

**Code churn / PR size:**
- `pr.additions`, `pr.deletions`, `pr.changed_files`, `pr.commits`
- Per-file: diff entry `additions`, `deletions`, `status`

**Review quality:**
- `review.state` distribution per reviewer
- `review_comments` count vs. `reviews` count
- Review thread `isResolved` rate
- Time between `review_requested` (timeline event) and `review.submitted_at`

**CI health:**
- Workflow run `conclusion` rates (success/failure/cancelled)
- `run_duration_ms` trends
- Job `started_at` → `completed_at` per runner
- Step-level timing for bottleneck detection

**Security hygiene:**
- Code scanning `severity` distribution, `fixed_at` presence
- Secret scanning `push_protection_bypassed` counts
- Dependabot `severity`, time-to-fix (`fixed_at` − `created_at`)
- Dependency review `vulnerabilities[]` on each PR diff

**Collaboration patterns:**
- `requested_reviewers` response time (GraphQL timeline)
- `author_association` of commenters
- `PullRequestReviewEvent` frequency per contributor
- Punch card data for work-hour analysis
